chunk_summary: Management of infants under six months with severe acute malnutrition focuses on breastfeeding assessment, danger signs, and referral thresholds, emphasizing intensive support and follow-up for mother–infant units.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for severe acute malnutrition in infants under six months, including weight-for-length and bilateral pitting edema.
Learn the steps for assessing breastfeeding effectiveness and implementing relactation strategies like Supplementary Suckling Technique (SST).
Identify danger signs requiring urgent referral or admission, such as lethargy, dehydration, or suspected sepsis.
Explore safe replacement feeding methods when breastfeeding is not feasible.
key_facts: Severe acute malnutrition in infants under six months is defined by very low weight-for-length and/or bilateral pitting edema.
Danger signs requiring urgent referral include lethargy, convulsions, severe respiratory distress, hypothermia, dehydration, and suspected sepsis.
Breastfeeding assessment includes latch, position, audible swallowing, feeding frequency, maternal pain, and use of bottles/teats.
Relactation strategies include Supplementary Suckling Technique (SST) and cup/spoon feeding of expressed breast milk (EBM).
Replacement feeding should be hygienic, affordable, and administered via cup and spoon when breastfeeding is not possible.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: WHO defines severe acute malnutrition in infants under six months by very low weight-for-length and/or bilateral pitting edema, framing them as 'infants at risk of poor growth and development.'
document_key_topics: Severe acute malnutrition criteria in infants under six months
Danger signs and urgent referral thresholds
